1: neum YHWH laadoni shev limini ad ashit oyvecha hadom leraglecha (nĕʾum Yhwh laʾdōnî, šēb liymînî ʿad-ʾāšît ʾōyĕbêkā hădōm lĕraglêkā): 'The oracle of YHWH to my lord: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool.' Neum (oracle of) is the formula for divine speech — the highest possible speech-authority marker in Hebrew prophecy. David writes laadoni (to my lord), creating the theological puzzle Jesus exploits in Matt 22:41-46: how can David's descendant also be David's lord? The Davidic Messiah must be more than a Davidic son; only divine sonship accounts for the double identity. Shev limini (sit at my right hand): enthronement at the supreme position of divine authority — the NT applies this to the resurrection/ascension as Christ's enthronement (Acts 2:34-35; Eph 1:20-21; Heb 1:3).
4: nishba YHWH velo yinachem atta kohen leolam al divrati malkitsedek (nišbaʿ Yhwh wĕlōʾ yinnāḥēm ʾattā-kōhēn lĕʿôlām ʿal-diḇrātî malkîṣedeq): 'YHWH has sworn and will not change his mind: You are a priest forever after the manner of Melchizedek.' The divine oath (nishba ... velo yinachem) makes this the most certain OT promise after the Abrahamic oath. Malkitsedek (Melchizedek, king of righteousness): the mysterious priest-king of Gen 14:18-20 who blessed Abraham and received a tithe — without genealogy, without recorded birth or death. Hebrews 7 uses the silence of Scripture about Melchizedek's origins as a typological argument: a priesthood with no recorded beginning or end points to an eternal priesthood.
2: מַטֵּה-עֻזְּךָ יִשְׁלַח יְהוָה מִצִּיּוֹן רְדֵה בְּקֶרֶב אֹיְבֶיךָ (maṭṭêh-ʿuzzĕkā yishlah YHWH miṣṣiyyôn rĕdêh bĕqereb ʾōyĕbêkā, "The LORD extends your powerful scepter from Zion: rule in the midst of your enemies!") — maṭṭêh-ʿuzzĕkā: the rod/scepter of your strength (the symbol of royal authority — Gen 49:10: "the scepter shall not depart from Judah"; Num 24:17: "a scepter shall rise from Israel"). The scepter is sent/stretched out from Zion (the place of the divine throne — Ps 2:6) into the midst of the enemies — not to a safe territory but to the enemy's own domain. Rĕdêh bĕqereb ʾōyĕbêkā: "rule in the midst of your enemies!" — the present-tense command to exercise dominion before the enemies are defeated. Sovereignty is exercised in the midst of opposition, not after its resolution (Rom 8:37: "in all these things we are more than conquerors").
3: עַמְּךָ נְדָבֹת בְּיוֹם חֵילֶךָ בְּהַדְרֵי-קֹדֶשׁ מֵרֶחֶם מִשְׁחָר לְךָ טַל יַלְדֻתֶיךָ (ʿammĕkā nĕdābōt bĕyôm ḥêlekā bĕhadĕrê-qōdesh mêreḥem mishḥar lĕkā ṭal yaldutêkā, "On the day you march to war, your people are freewill-offerings, in holy splendor; like dew from the womb of the dawn, your young warriors come to you!") — nĕdābōt: freewill-offerings/volunteers (from nādab: to volunteer, to give freely — Judg 5:2: "when the leaders led and the people volunteered, bless the LORD!"; Exod 35:21,29: the free-will giving for the tabernacle). The army is not conscripted but volunteer — those who come because they want to, as freewill offerings. Bĕhadĕrê-qōdesh: in holy splendors (the sacred adornment — Ps 29:2; 96:9; 1 Chr 16:29). Mêreḥem mishḥar lĕkā ṭal yaldutêkā: "from the womb of the dawn, like dew, is your youth/young warriors" — the dawn's womb generating dew as rapidly and abundantly as the Messiah's army materializes: innumerable, fresh, and forming before anyone expected them (the dew's miraculous appearance).
5: אֲדֹנָי עַל-יְמִינְךָ מָחַץ בְּיוֹם-אַפּוֹ מְלָכִים (ʾădōnāy ʿal-yĕmînĕkā māḥaṣ bĕyôm-ʾappô mĕlākhîm, "The Lord stands at your right hand; he will crush kings on the day of his wrath!") — a remarkable inversion: in v1, the Messiah sits at YHWH's right hand; here, ʾădōnāy (the Lord) is at the Messiah's right hand. The two verses together describe a dynamic royal relationship: the Messiah is exalted to YHWH's right hand (v1), and YHWH supports the Messiah at his right hand (v5) when he goes into battle. Māḥaṣ bĕyôm-ʾappô mĕlākhîm: he shatters/crushes kings on the day of his wrath (the divine anger making the enthroned Messiah's enemies the target — Ps 2:12: "kiss the Son, lest he be angry"). The day of wrath is the day of royal judgment.
6: יָדִין בַּגּוֹיִם מָלֵא גְוִיּוֹת מָחַץ רֹאשׁ עַל-אֶרֶץ רַבָּה (yādîn baggôyim māllêʾ gĕwiyyôt māḥaṣ rōʾsh ʿal-ʾereṣ rabbāh, "He will bring judgment on the nations, filling the land with the dead; he will crush the ruler across the wide earth!") — yādîn baggôyim: he executes judgment among the nations (the final worldwide judgment — the scope of the Messiah's authority established in Ps 2:8: "I will give you the nations as your inheritance"). Māllêʾ gĕwiyyôt: filling with corpses (gĕwiyyāh: body/corpse — the bodies of the defeated). Māḥaṣ rōʾsh: he shatters the head (the decisive blow to the singular ruler — rōʾsh as the leadership representative — Hab 3:13: "you crushed the head of the house of evil"). The crushing of the head recalls Gen 3:15: "he will crush your head."
7: מִנַּחַל בַּדֶּרֶךְ יִשְׁתֶּה עַל-כֵּן יָרִים רֹאשׁ (minnaḥal badderek yishteh ʿal-kên yārîm rōʾsh, "He drinks from the stream along the road, and because of this he will hold his head high!") — the enigmatic final verse of Psalm 110. Minnaḥal badderek yishteh: "from the wadi/stream in the road he drinks" — mid-pursuit, the warrior-king refreshes himself from a stream rather than stopping to make camp. This is the image of relentless pursuit that does not pause for full provision. The parallel is Gideon's 300 who lapped water like dogs and kept moving (Judg 7:5-7 — the select few who did not kneel to drink). ʿAl-kên yārîm rōʾsh: "therefore he will lift up his head" (rûm rōʾsh: to raise the head — the gesture of triumph and vindication — Ps 3:3: "you lift up my head"; Gen 40:13,20 — the butler's head lifted in restoration). The king who drinks from the stream in the way is refreshed for the final victory.
